content: Hi folks! I'm learning more about Rust for Linux recently and I was wondering if you guys have plans to include bindings for the include/linux/dma-buf.h and include/linux/dma-resv.h files.

If your question implies you want to work on it, then go ahead ;-) And we are happy to help you need anything.

The bindings we have right now are the ones required by some drivers in Rust that people are working on. Of course your interest in the dma-buf bindings may suggest you have some usages I think ;-)

Note that at the end of day, it's likely that it's up to dma-buf maintainers in kernel for the acceptance of the bindings. Therefore, it's not a bad idea to ask for their inputs as early as possible.
I see. Thanks for the feedback!
I worked on some dma buffer support for the NVMe driver. The work is not merged in rust or rust-next, but @Andreas Hindborg is maintaining it
Do you have a link to the repo?

From the deck: https://lpc.events/event/16/contributions/1180/attachments/1017/1961/deck.pdf
I think at least there is an the old branch: https://github.com/metaspace/linux/tree/nvme
Thanks!

Boqun Feng said:

From the deck: https://lpc.events/event/16/contributions/1180/attachments/1017/1961/deck.pdf
I think at least there is an the old branch: https://github.com/metaspace/linux/tree/nvme

@Maíra Canal that is the most up to date work. I had to step away from this for a while, but I am back working on it. There is also an e1000 driver that uses dma abstractions https://github.com/fujita/rust-e1000. This branch only contain the driver module, not sure what tree he builds against.

I am in the process of rebasing the nvme driver on recent linux release. It's a bit of a mess because parts of the rust branch are now merged upstream. It's not exactly the same code though. For instance PointerWrapper is ForeignOwnable upstream. Upstreamed patches have also gone through additional review and is no longer exactly the same code that is in rust. I think its just going to be a slow manual process. @Miguel Ojeda any ideas for the most efficient way to proceed? (sorry for hijacking the topic)
I'm going to push a rename in rust as well
(if you could wait a couple of days, I think your life would be easier)
I basically need to rebase (parts of) the rust branch on top of rust-next i guess.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I basically need to rebase (parts of) the rust branch on top of rust-next i guess.

Oh, I think you first wanted to sync with rust before going to rust-next. Going to rust-next first is going to be much harder because we still have a lot of missing dependencies there

Maíra Canal that is the most up to date work. I had to step away from this for a while, but I am back working on it. There is also an e1000 driver that uses dma abstractions https://github.com/fujita/rust-e1000. This branch only contain the driver module, not sure what tree he builds against.

Thanks for the link! Do you guys have any suggestions of good beginner's issues? Most of the "good first issues" are already done.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I basically need to rebase (parts of) the rust branch on top of rust-next i guess.

Oh, I think you first wanted to sync with rust before going to rust-next. Going to rust-next first is going to be much harder because we still have a lot of missing dependencies there

What is the plan for the rust branch as more and more bits go upstream? It would be super awesome if you kept it on top of latest kernel release.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I basically need to rebase (parts of) the rust branch on top of rust-next i guess.

Oh, I think you first wanted to sync with rust before going to rust-next. Going to rust-next first is going to be much harder because we still have a lot of missing dependencies there

What is the plan for the rust branch as more and more bits go upstream? It would be super awesome if you kept it on top of latest kernel release.

It's a ton of work, but it is work that would need to be done anyway as the pieces go upstream right?

What is the plan for the rust branch as more and more bits go upstream? It would be super awesome if you kept it on top of latest kernel release.

Yeah, I am happy to make rust match rust-next as closely as possible (and thus mainline).
(This is why we asked the other day whether it was useful for any of you working downstream at the moment or not)

Andreas Hindborg said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

I basically need to rebase (parts of) the rust branch on top of rust-next i guess.

Oh, I think you first wanted to sync with rust before going to rust-next. Going to rust-next first is going to be much harder because we still have a lot of missing dependencies there

What is the plan for the rust branch as more and more bits go upstream? It would be super awesome if you kept it on top of latest kernel release.

It's a ton of work, but it is work that would need to be done anyway as the pieces go upstream right?

No, not necessarily. Our plan is bring the two closer and closer. We know some things in rust will never be upstreamed as they are, but once we upstream all your dependencies, your work will be for your own pieces, and they should fit neatly as the dependencies will already be there.

We discussed this in one of the meetings: do we want to keep rust in sync with rust-next or should we let it rot? I think the consensus now is that we will keep it in sync

It's a ton of work, but it is work that would need to be done anyway as the pieces go upstream right?

It needs to be done either way when sending one of the pieces upstream, indeed, but maintaining the branch is still some more work, because it needs taking care of both sides rather than one (e.g. new changes to Rust stuff landing in mainline directly, removing outdated things in rust, back merging LInus' branch (i.e. new changes to non-Rust stuff), etc.).

On your side (Andreas), you will still need to do merging/rebasing to keep track of the newer rusts, and do yet more rebases at the end to prepare the final patches to submit to rust-next etc.

What maintaining the rust branch allows us (and you if you keep merging/rebasing on top of it) is to see what is left more clearly, and for you to move back and forth from rust to mainline when you get closer, etc.

Miguel Ojeda said:

On your side (Andreas), you will still need to do merging/rebasing to keep track of the newer rusts, and do yet more rebases at the end to prepare the final patches to submit to rust-next etc.

That is super!
Trying to move on top of rust-next has proven to be a bit of a battle. On top of rust sounds more manageable and makes sense if rust is kept up to date.

I've started building up the DRM abstractions on top of rust-next here: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/952 (turns out it doesn't need that much!).

We'll still need a bunch more things from rust for real drivers, but at this point I think it's more productive to get what we need rebased on rust-next (even if it involves some ugly temporary commits that someone else will have to pick apart before upstreaming) rather than trying to work on rust, since I think merging back changes from upstream is going to be increasingly painful and makes it very difficult to see what is actually missing from upstream...

I also started working on the related dma-fence abstraction last week, but it's not pushed anywhere yet since it's completely untested. I'll probably get somewhere enough to push it by the end of this coming week ^^

Asahi Lina said:

I also started working on the related dma-fence abstraction last week, but it's not pushed anywhere yet since it's completely untested. I'll probably get somewhere enough to push it by the end of this coming week ^^

Are you planning to implement dma_fence_ops?
Possibly, I haven't gotten far enough yet to figure out what I need.
Nice work! Personally I hope that drm can be a standalone in-kernel crate ;-)

Yes, we need separate crates so they can be built as modules too, I'm looking forward to the plumbing for that ^^

It shouldn't be a blocker if it takes longer though, since it's not a big deal if a single driver hard-depends on CONFIG_DRM=y and a few other things.
yeah, agreed it’s not a big deal

Also I noticed a funny thing... for some things it works anyway. I made my RTKit abstraction hard-depend on y there, but I didn't have that originally and it still worked. I think it's because the whole abstraction is generic over a driver implementation, so the code never actually gets linked into the kernel binary, but rather the driver crate that uses it monomorphizes it and so the module symbols work out!
We've been shipping CONFIG_APPLE_RTKIT=m all along without noticing to everyone, even though that abstraction was right in the main kernel crate ^^

Asahi Lina said:

We'll still need a bunch more things from rust for real drivers, but at this point I think it's more productive to get what we need rebased on rust-next (even if it involves some ugly temporary commits that someone else will have to pick apart before upstreaming) rather than trying to work on rust, since I think merging back changes from upstream is going to be increasingly painful and makes it very difficult to see what is actually missing from upstream...

If we merge upstream back into rust it will be difficult. If we rebase the remaining changes in rust on upstream it will be easy to see what is not yet merged.

I moved the nvme driver on top of rust for now. Picking changes in rust that I need and rebasing those on top of rust-next is a hassle, and something that the original authors of those changes need to do anyway when those changes are sent upstream. I will probably not do it the way the original author would and then we have double work.
